meta.title: Multi-Agent Orchestration
meta.sub: Literature explorer · 92 papers, 5 themes · SciX MCP (arXiv + ADS) + industry sources · built 2026-06-08
meta.what_is: An opinionated map of the LLM multi-agent orchestration literature, from the 2023 framework wave through the 2026 reliability frontier. It was assembled with the SciX MCP server (NASA ADS + arXiv mirror) as a seed, fanned out across five sub-agent deep dives, and stitched together with vendor and industry engineering sources where peer-reviewed coverage is thin.
meta.caveat: The SciX dense/ANN vector index was down during collection, so corpus recall is lexical/hybrid only — treat coverage as a floor, not a census. Many 2025–2026 entries are low- or zero-citation preprints presented as emerging vocabulary, not settled fact. Production-engineering knowledge (observability, SRE-for-agents, incident postmortems) is drawn mostly from vendor/industry blogs because it is near-absent from peer review.
start_here: ★ Reading path. Start with the Guo 2024 survey for the taxonomy, then AutoGen / MetaGPT / Generative Agents for the founding patterns, then the 2025–2026 scaling, failure, and reliability work that defines the frontier. See the Reading list for a 12-paper onboarding order.

★ Opportunities. Twelve open problems — causal failure attribution, verified coordination, cost-bounded orchestration, durable team memory, and more — are catalogued under Opportunities, each now with a named 2026 system attacking it.
Design deep-dives. Three keystone systems (AutoGen, Magentic-One, Generative Agents) are dissected for task design, evidence, key numbers, and what is reusable in your own builds.
Timeline. The Temporal view traces the 2023 framework explosion, the 2024–2025 consolidation, and the 2026 turn toward reliability, security, and cost.
Themes (left nav). Five threads: Foundations & Topologies, Patterns & Frameworks, Memory in MAS, Enterprise & Production, and Frontier & Open Problems.
meta.one_paragraph: Multi-agent orchestration began in 2023 as a framework gold-rush — AutoGen's conversable agents, MetaGPT's role SOPs, ChatDev's chat-chain, CAMEL's role-play, and Du's multi-agent debate established that decomposition, specialization, and cross-checking could push LLMs past single-agent ceilings, while Park's Generative Agents proved memory-driven societies could behave believably. By 2024–2025 the question shifted from 'can agents collaborate' to 'should they, and how': scaling studies split into an optimist camp (MacNet's logistic collaborative scaling law over 1000+ agents) and a pessimist camp (SIMAS's non-monotonic diminishing returns), the MAST taxonomy named why systems fail, and Anthropic's production write-up quantified the cost — a 90.2% quality win at ~15× the token spend. 2026 is the reliability reckoning: the field now has hard numbers on security (architecture itself is the attack surface; stronger workers can be *less* safe), cost (affine-typed budgets, cost-aware cascades), memory (active broadcast over passive blackboards, fast-acquire/slow-consolidate), and observability (OpenTelemetry GenAI conventions), reframing orchestration as the classical distributed-systems problem of making stochastic components behave like a dependable system.
meta.podcast: Companion to the five-episode Code Intel Digest — Multi-Agent Orchestration deep-dive series (Foundations & Topologies; Patterns & Frameworks; Memory in Multi-Agent Systems; Enterprise & Production; Frontier & Open Problems).
meta.design_sub: Deep dives into keystone systems: design, evidence, and what is reusable.

papers.title: LLM-based Multi-Agents: A Survey of Progress and Challenges

notes.k: Canonical survey of LLM multi-agent systems with the field's reference taxonomy of architectures, coordination patterns, and challenges.
notes.r: The single best entry point; the map every other paper is read against.

papers.title: AutoGen: Enabling Next-Gen LLM Applications via Multi-Agent Conversation

notes.k: Introduces the conversable-agent abstraction: agents that send and receive messages, with configurable human and tool participation, composed into flexible conversation patterns.
notes.r: The most-cited framework anchor; its primitives recur in every later vendor SDK (now absorbed into Microsoft Agent Framework).

papers.title: MetaGPT: Meta Programming for a Multi-Agent Collaborative Framework

notes.k: Encodes Standard Operating Procedures and a shared message pool so role agents publish structured artifacts (PRDs, designs, code) instead of free chat.
notes.r: Hardened role specialization into an org-chart program; its shared message pool is the lineage of the passive-blackboard team-memory pattern.

papers.title: Improving Factuality and Reasoning in Language Models through Multiagent Debate

notes.k: Multiple LLM instances propose, critique, and revise across rounds; factuality and reasoning improve over single-model answers.
notes.r: Origin of the debate-improves-reasoning lineage that later work both extends (sparse topologies) and rebuts (cost of consensus).

papers.title: ChatDev: Communicative Agents for Software Development

notes.k: Organizes a virtual software company as a chat-chain of phase-scoped two-agent dialogues (design, code, test, document).
notes.r: Society-of-mind made operational; a foundational role-specialization system for software tasks.

papers.title: CAMEL: Communicative Agents for Mind Exploration of LLM Society

notes.k: Introduces inception/role-play prompting: an AI-user and an AI-assistant prompt each other into staying on task without human turn-by-turn steering.
notes.r: The oldest role-specialization pattern in the corpus; seeds the autonomous role-play paradigm.

papers.title: AgentVerse: Facilitating Multi-Agent Collaboration and Exploring Emergent Behaviors

notes.k: A framework for assembling collaborating role agents that studies the emergent collaborative behaviors arising when agents work as a group.
notes.r: Early evidence that group dynamics, not just individual capability, shape multi-agent outcomes.

papers.title: Multi-Agent Collaboration: Harnessing the Power of Intelligent LLM Agents

notes.k: A blueprint for coordinating intelligent LLM agents, naming the building blocks of collaboration and control.
notes.r: An early coordination blueprint that frames the design space the topology zoo later fills in.

papers.title: Dynamic LLM-Powered Agent Network for Task-Oriented Agent Collaboration (DyLAN)

notes.k: Activates and connects agents dynamically based on the task rather than a fixed wiring, with an inference-time agent-importance score for team selection.
notes.r: The corpus anchor for dynamic topology / routing — the precursor to adaptive, query-conditioned orchestration.

papers.title: Improving Multi-Agent Debate with Sparse Communication Topology

notes.k: Shows that all-to-all debate is wasteful: a sparse communication topology preserves most of the reasoning benefit at a fraction of the token cost.
notes.r: The standing rebuttal to dense-debate defaults; couples topology choice to cost directly.

papers.title: Magentic-One: A Generalist Multi-Agent System for Solving Complex Tasks

notes.k: Reference orchestrator-worker architecture: a lead Orchestrator maintains task and progress ledgers, dispatches to specialist workers (web surfer, coder, file navigator), and re-plans when stuck.
notes.r: The canonical production topology and the ledger pattern that defines shared working state in a team.

papers.title: Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail? (MAST)

notes.k: First systematic 14-category taxonomy of why multi-agent systems fail, built by hand-annotating real traces; failures span specification, inter-agent misalignment, and verification.
notes.r: Gives the field named failure modes and shows most production breakage is coordination, not base-model cognition.

papers.title: MALT: Improving Reasoning with Multi-Agent LLM Training

notes.k: Trains specialized generator, verifier, and refiner roles jointly rather than relying on prompted role-play.
notes.r: Pushes role specialization from prompting toward trained division of labor.

papers.title: MACI: Multi-Agent Collaborative Intelligence for Adaptive Reasoning and Temporal Planning

notes.k: A meta-planner coordinates collaborative agents for adaptive reasoning and temporal planning over constraints.
notes.r: Treats planning itself as a coordinated meta-layer rather than a single agent's job.

papers.title: S-Agents: Self-organizing Agents in Open-ended Environments

notes.k: Self-organizes agents into a tree-of-agents structure for open-ended environments without a fixed central controller.
notes.r: A swarm/decentralized point in the topology zoo; coordination emerges from local structure.

papers.title: Hierarchical Language Agent for Real-time Human-AI Coordination

notes.k: A hierarchical agent architecture that meets real-time latency constraints in human-AI coordination.
notes.r: Shows hierarchy as a lever for latency and responsiveness, not just organizational tidiness.

papers.title: DynTaskMAS: Dynamic Task-Graph LLM Multi-Agent System

notes.k: Decomposes work into an asynchronous task-graph so independent sub-tasks run in parallel rather than in a serial chat.
notes.r: Moves orchestration from chat sequences toward true parallel dataflow.

papers.title: AgentNet: Decentralized Evolutionary Coordination for LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems

notes.k: A decentralized scheme where agents evolve their connections over time, adapting the coordination graph without central control.
notes.r: An evolutionary take on decentralized topology — the network learns its own wiring.

papers.title: Coordination as an Architectural Layer for LLM Multi-Agent Systems

notes.k: Argues coordination should be a first-class architectural layer rather than an emergent side effect of prompting.
notes.r: Names the design move the whole field is converging on: make coordination explicit and inspectable.

papers.title: Cayley Graph Optimization for Scalable MAS Communication Topologies

notes.k: Uses Cayley-graph structure to design communication topologies that scale efficiently as agent count grows.
notes.r: A principled, mathematically-grounded approach to the topology-scalability problem.

papers.title: A Two-Dimensional Framework for AI Agent Design Patterns

notes.k: Organizes the space of agent design patterns along two axes, producing a structured pattern taxonomy.
notes.r: The right citation for 'here is the map of all the patterns' — a modern pattern catalogue.

papers.title: TraceFix: Repairing Agent Coordination Protocols with TLA+

notes.k: Models an agent coordination protocol in TLA+ and automatically finds and repairs coordination bugs the way model checkers find deadlocks in distributed protocols.
notes.r: The most direct import of classical formal methods; partial answer to 'can we prove a handoff is safe.'

papers.title: MPAC: Multi-Principal Agent Coordination Protocol

notes.k: A multi-principal coordination layer that sits above tool/messaging protocols like MCP and A2A to coordinate agents serving different principals.
notes.r: Addresses interoperability and trust when agents answer to multiple owners.

papers.title: ARIADNE: Blackboard-Driven Monte-Carlo Tree Search for Multi-Agent Reasoning

notes.k: Drives a Monte-Carlo tree search over a shared blackboard that agents read and write rather than messaging each other directly.
notes.r: A modern instantiation of the classic blackboard/shared-workspace architecture; sits on the passive-substrate side of the team-memory debate.

papers.title: Self-Evolving Multi-Agent Systems via Decentralized Memory

notes.k: Fuses agent-memory and MAS: persistent decentralized team state lets a multi-agent system self-evolve, weighing shared versus per-agent memory.
notes.r: Directly bridges the agentic-memory and multi-agent series — team state as the substrate of self-improvement.

papers.title: SHIMI: Semantic Hierarchical Memory Index for Collective Agent Memory

notes.k: A semantic hierarchical memory index used as collective memory, synchronized across nodes via a Merkle-DAG + CRDT scheme so the shared store converges without a central coordinator.
notes.r: Shows collective memory can be both structured and decentralized-consistent at once.

papers.title: iAgents: Collaborative Task Completion under Information Asymmetry

notes.k: Studies what each agent knows versus what it must communicate when information is asymmetrically distributed across a team.
notes.r: Reframes communication as the explicit transfer of privately-held knowledge — a core team-memory question.

papers.title: DroidSpeak: KV Cache Sharing for Cross-LLM Communication

notes.k: When agents share a base model, KV caches can be shared across them to cut cross-LLM communication cost — shared memory at the attention-state level.
notes.r: The lowest, most literal tier of shared memory; trades text-passing for substrate-level state.

papers.title: LCGuard: Safe KV Cache Sharing in Multi-Agent Systems

notes.k: Treats shared KV-cache representations as an attack surface and guards them, the safety counterweight to DroidSpeak-style sharing.
notes.r: Shows that the cheapest shared-memory tier is also a new security boundary.

papers.title: IBGP: Imperfect Byzantine Generals Problem for Multi-Agent Robustness

notes.k: Frames error propagation in agent teams as an imperfect Byzantine-generals consensus problem.
notes.r: Imports classical fault-tolerance theory to reason about how bad outputs spread and can be contained.

papers.title: ACIArena: Evaluating Agent Cascading Injection

notes.k: A benchmark for agent cascading injection — how an injected instruction propagates through shared context from one agent to the next.
notes.r: Quantifies error/attack propagation via the shared substrate, the dark side of team memory.

papers.title: Aligned Agents, Biased Swarm: Bias Amplification in Multi-Agent Systems

notes.k: Shows individually aligned agents can produce a biased swarm: bias is amplified through memory-mediated interaction even when each agent is fine.
notes.r: Refutes any deployment story built on 'each agent is individually safe'; per-agent alignment does not compose.

papers.title: Topology and Memory of Consensus among LLM Agents

notes.k: Studies how communication topology and agent memory jointly shape whether a team reaches consensus.
notes.r: Makes explicit that memory and topology interact — neither can be tuned in isolation.

papers.title: The Dynamics of Social Conventions in LLM Populations

notes.k: Documents how social conventions emerge from accumulated interaction memory in populations of LLM agents.
notes.r: Evidence that norms precipitate out of a shared memory substrate over time.

papers.title: Spontaneous Emergence of Agent Individuality through Social Interactions in LLM-Based Communities

notes.k: Distinct agent identities emerge spontaneously from accumulated interaction memory in LLM communities.
notes.r: Identity, like convention, is shown to be a downstream product of memory accumulation.

papers.title: Agent Workflow Memory

notes.k: Distills reusable procedural routines (workflows) from past trajectories so agents stop re-deriving the same multi-step procedures.
notes.r: Seeds the procedural/workflow tier of team memory that later shared-repo systems scale across users.

papers.title: Encouraging Divergent Thinking in LLMs through Multi-Agent Debate

notes.k: Uses multi-agent debate to counter the degeneration-of-thought problem and encourage divergent reasoning.
notes.r: A high-citation debate result showing communication itself is a coordination mechanism against premature convergence.

papers.title: ReConcile: Round-Table Conference Improves Reasoning via Consensus among Diverse LLMs

notes.k: A round-table consensus protocol among diverse LLMs that share deliberation and revise toward agreement.
notes.r: An early structured-consensus method and shared-deliberation-memory pattern.

papers.title: AutoGen Studio: A No-Code Developer Tool for Building and Debugging Multi-Agent Systems

notes.k: A no-code tool for building, debugging, and inspecting multi-agent message flow and state on top of AutoGen.
notes.r: Tooling for the message-flow/state layer — making coordination observable to builders.

papers.title: Multi-Agent Design: Optimizing Agents with Better Prompts and Topologies

notes.k: Jointly optimizes prompts and communication topologies, showing topology is a first-class lever on performance, not a deployment afterthought.
notes.r: Independent corroboration that the wiring, held model fixed, drives outcomes.

papers.title: Decentralized Generative Agents with Adaptive Hierarchical Knowledge Graph

notes.k: Maintains a shared, adaptive hierarchical knowledge graph as collective memory so structured facts persist and compose across agents.
notes.r: A structured-knowledge tier of team memory — collective memory as a queryable graph.

papers.title: Project Sid: Many-Agent Simulations toward AI Civilization

notes.k: Scales generative agents to many-agent civilization simulations with institutional memory: laws, roles, and culture that persist beyond any one agent.
notes.r: The institutional-memory tier — conventions and identity at population scale.

papers.title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior

notes.k: Establishes the memory-stream-plus-reflection architecture: agents record observations, retrieve by relevance/recency/importance, and synthesize higher-level reflections.
notes.r: The memory anchor of the whole field and the crossover point with the agentic-memory series; believable society from memory alone.

papers.title: Emergent Communication in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Enhances Foraging

notes.k: Pre-LLM result: emergent communication via stigmergy enhances collective foraging in multi-agent RL.
notes.r: Roots externalized, environment-mediated coordination (stigmergy) that LLM swarms re-instantiate.

papers.title: PheroCom: Virtual Pheromone-based Communication for Swarm Coordination

notes.k: Coordinates a swarm through virtual pheromones — externalized collective memory written into a shared field.
notes.r: A classic externalized-memory coordination mechanism; ancestor of blackboard/shared-field designs.

papers.title: Robust Multi-Agent LLMs under Byzantine Faults

notes.k: Studies and improves reliability of multi-agent LLM systems when some agents are Byzantine-faulty (arbitrarily wrong or adversarial).
notes.r: Brings distributed-systems fault tolerance to agent teams as a reliability discipline.

papers.title: From Debate to Decision: Conformal Social Choice for Multi-Agent Consensus

notes.k: Applies conformal prediction to multi-agent consensus, giving a principled abstention threshold so the system can decline rather than ratify confidently-wrong agreement.
notes.r: Separates agreement from correctness — a foundation for calibrated consensus and safe abstention.

papers.title: Governance by Construction for Generalist Agents

notes.k: Compiles policy-as-code with human-in-the-loop checkpoints so governance is built into the agent's execution path by construction.
notes.r: Operationalizes governance as a compile-time/runtime guarantee rather than a review afterthought.

papers.title: AGrail: A Lifelong Agent Guardrail with Effective and Adaptive Safety Detection

notes.k: A lifelong, adaptive runtime guardrail that updates its safety detection over the agent's operational lifetime.
notes.r: Moves guardrails from static filters to adaptive, continuously-learning runtime controls.

papers.title: Current State of LLM Risks and AI Guardrails

notes.k: Surveys LLM risks and the landscape of AI guardrail techniques.
notes.r: A reference map of the guardrail design space production teams draw from.

papers.title: Prompt Infection: LLM-to-LLM Prompt Injection within Multi-Agent Systems

notes.k: Demonstrates agent-to-agent prompt injection: a malicious instruction launders itself through one agent's output into another's input.
notes.r: Names the core multi-agent attack — the architecture itself becomes the attack surface.

papers.title: Agent Security Bench (ASB): Formalizing and Benchmarking Attacks and Defenses in LLM-based Agents

notes.k: A benchmark formalizing attacks and defenses across the LLM-agent attack surface (prompt injection, memory poisoning, tool misuse).
notes.r: Gives the field a shared yardstick for agent security claims.

papers.title: A Reward Hacking Benchmark for Tool-Use Agents

notes.k: Benchmarks spec-gaming / reward hacking in tool-use agents — agents satisfying the letter of a goal while violating intent.
notes.r: Targets the silent failure where an agent's tool call is syntactically perfect but semantically wrong.

papers.title: CodeTracer: Traceable Agent States for Debugging

notes.k: Makes agent states traceable so multi-agent runs can be reconstructed and debugged step by step.
notes.r: The research counterpart to OpenTelemetry's operational standard for replayable agent traces.

papers.title: Acceptance-Test-Driven Evaluation Protocols for Agents

notes.k: Proposes acceptance-test-driven evaluation protocols for enterprise agents, replacing ad hoc demos with pre-agreed pass/fail criteria.
notes.r: Brings software-engineering acceptance testing to agent evaluation — the basis for 'earned autonomy' thresholds.

papers.title: CompliBench: LLM Judges for Compliance Violation Detection

notes.k: A benchmark for using LLM judges to detect compliance violations in agent behavior.
notes.r: Targets the compliance-guardrail evaluation gap for regulated deployments.

papers.title: Model Context Protocol (MCP): Landscape, Security Threats, and Future Directions

notes.k: Surveys the MCP tool-integration protocol landscape and its security threats (tool poisoning, confused-deputy, supply-chain risk).
notes.r: The corpus anchor for the tool-integration substrate every agent stack now depends on.

papers.title: CascadeDebate: Cost-Aware LLM Cascades for Multi-Agent Debate

notes.k: Cost-aware cascades that escalate to expensive models only when cheaper ones disagree, controlling debate's compute blow-up.
notes.r: A direct attack on the cost axis of orchestration via cognitive-tier routing.

papers.title: ATOM: Adaptive Nucleus-Electron Orchestration for Multi-Agent Systems

notes.k: An adaptive orchestrator with a stable offline-learned nucleus and query-conditioned worker electrons activated only when difficulty warrants; names the 'average-complexity trap' of static dense topologies.
notes.r: Shows topology should bend to task difficulty — up to 30% token savings by right-sizing the team per query.

papers.title: Predictive Maps of Multi-Agent Reasoning: Spectral Diagnostics of Topology

notes.k: Derives closed-form spectra of the communication operator that map to three failure modes, giving a pre-deployment diagnostic of a topology.
notes.r: Reframes 'pick a topology' from taste to engineering: condition number, spectral gap, and radius predict robustness, consensus, and drift.

papers.title: Scaling Large Language Model-based Multi-Agent Collaboration (MacNet)

notes.k: Organizes 1000+ agents as a directed acyclic graph and finds a collaborative scaling law: performance follows a logistic growth curve, with irregular topologies beating regular ones (chain, star, tree, complete).
notes.r: The optimist pole of the scaling debate; collaborative emergence appears earlier than neural-scaling emergence.

papers.title: Scaling Behavior of Single LLM-Driven Multi-Agent Systems (SIMAS)

notes.k: Isolates the effect of adding agents from model/knowledge heterogeneity and finds MAS performance does not scale monotonically — diminishing returns governed by a synergy-versus-coordination-overhead trade-off.
notes.r: The pessimist pole of the scaling debate; collective intelligence is contingent on interaction design, not agent count.

papers.title: Embodied LLM Agents Learn to Cooperate in Organized Teams

notes.k: Flat LLM teams over-report and over-comply, creating redundancy and confusion; imposing prompt-based organizational structure with designated leadership improves efficiency and cuts communication cost.
notes.r: Empirical case that hierarchy is a lever to suppress chatter, not just an org-chart box.

papers.title: MMA2A: Multi-Modal Agent-to-Agent Routing and the Information Topology

notes.k: On CrossModal-CS, modality-native routing reached 52% task completion vs 32% for a text-bottleneck baseline with the same backend — but only when an LLM (not keyword matching) consumed the richer context.
notes.r: The sharpest ablation in the corpus: the pattern, not the model, is the variable, and richer protocols buy nothing without a reasoning layer that can exploit them.

papers.title: Agents in Software Engineering: Survey, Landscape, and Vision

notes.k: Surveys SE agents with a clean perception / memory / action three-module mental model for what each role agent is.
notes.r: The cleanest abstraction for reasoning about a role agent before naming any framework.

papers.title: OpenHands: An Open Platform for AI Software Developers as Generalist Agents

notes.k: A generalist code-agent platform (formerly OpenDevin) with explicit multi-agent coordination and sandboxed execution.
notes.r: The bridge from coordination patterns to systems developers actually run.

papers.title: SOEN-101 / FlowGen: Code Generation via Software-Process Models with Multi-Agent

notes.k: Instantiates Waterfall, TDD, and Scrum as multi-agent topologies with the same role agents re-wired into different communication patterns, yielding different code quality.
notes.r: The vivid demonstration that the org chart is literally the program.

papers.title: Beyond Individual Intelligence: The LIFE Progression of Multi-Agent Capability

notes.k: A LIFE-progression taxonomy (Lay capability, Integrate, Find faults, Evolve) that unifies collaboration, failure-attribution, and self-evolution into one structural arc.
notes.r: Gives the maturity arc a vocabulary — from doing the task to evolving the team.

papers.title: OneManCompany: Dynamically Recruited Agent Workforces from a Talent Market

notes.k: Packages skills, tools, and config into portable 'Talent' identities recruited on demand from a Talent Market, decoupling org structure from what an agent knows.
notes.r: Fixed crews give way to dynamically recruited workforces — org as a separate, governable concern.

papers.title: Theater of Mind for LLMs: From Blackboard to the Global Workspace

notes.k: A naive two-agent loop over a passive shared pool rapidly descends into cognitive stagnation; Global Workspace Agents replace polling with an active broadcast hub, an entropy-based diversity drive, and bifurcated short-term/long-term memory.
notes.r: The hinge result for team memory: passive sharing fails; memory must be actively broadcast, curated, and consolidated.

papers.title: SkillClaw: Shared Synchronized Workflow Memory across a Multi-User Agent Ecosystem

notes.k: Aggregates cross-user trajectories into a shared, synchronized workflow-memory repo; an autonomous evolver turns recurring patterns into skill refinements that propagate system-wide.
notes.r: Stops workflows and failure modes from being repeatedly rediscovered per user — procedural memory at ecosystem scale.

papers.title: M*: Every Task Deserves Its Own Memory Harness

notes.k: Argues a memory system optimized for one purpose fails to transfer, and uses reflective code-evolution to auto-discover a task-specialized memory program (Schema + Logic + Instructions).
notes.r: The contrarian counterweight to one-shared-substrate: per-task specialization as a live, unsolved design axis.

papers.title: Governance by Design: A Parsonian Institutional Audit of an Agent Ecosystem

notes.k: A Parsonian governance audit of a large agent ecosystem (250k+ stars, 770k+ agents, 2M+ users) finds <=19% governance coverage and zero of twelve coordination pathways functional.
notes.r: Macro-scale rebuttal to 'conventions emerge automatically'; shared substrate is necessary but not sufficient without governance.

papers.title: Auto-Dreamer: CLS-based Fast Acquisition and Slow Consolidation of Agent Memory

notes.k: Invokes Complementary Learning Systems theory to decouple fast per-session memory acquisition from slow cross-session consolidation; a learned offline consolidator synthesizes compact replacements that supersede bloated regions.
notes.r: Makes records-management-as-consolidation executable: +7 on ScienceWorld with a 12x smaller memory bank, transferring without retraining.

papers.title: Unified Context Evolution (UCE): A Typed Library of Evolvable Context Units

notes.k: A gradient-free typed library of Evolvable Context Units (Memory, Strategy, Workflow, Skill), each generated from trajectories, quality-scored through use, and pruned when no longer valuable.
notes.r: Records-management governance for a team substrate: typing + quality tracking + retention carry the gains (ALFWorld 75.4%->96.3%).

papers.title: Architecture Matters: Security in Multi-Agent Systems

notes.k: Across three environments and 13 configurations, multi-agent setups were more vulnerable than single agents in most configs, with attack-success rates varying up to 3.8x at equal-or-higher benign accuracy; no design was universally safer.
notes.r: Kills the intuition that decomposition is inherently a defense — the architecture itself is the security variable.

papers.title: Cross-Session Threats: Memoryless Guardrails in Multi-Agent Systems

notes.k: An attacker who spreads a single attack across dozens of sessions evades every session-bound detector; session judges and full-log correlators lose ~half their recall, recovered by a bounded Coreset Memory Reader (K=50).
notes.r: Shows guardrails must be stateful across sessions, not just within one; releases CSTM-Bench.

papers.title: Context-Fragmented Policy Violations in Multi-Agent Workflows

notes.k: Each agent's action is locally safe but the actions collectively violate policy because policy facts are siloed across departments; eight frontier LLMs showed 14-98% violation rates, and a Distributed Sentinel with Semantic Taint Tokens reached F1=0.95 at 106 ms.
notes.r: Formalizes the confused-deputy export incident; guardrails must be distributed and zero-trust, not a monolithic gate.

papers.title: GAMMAF: Graph-based Anomaly Monitoring of Multi-Agent Debates

notes.k: Models MAS debates as attributed graphs and benchmarks graph-based anomaly detectors that isolate flagged adversarial nodes live.
notes.r: Bridges security and observability — treats the interaction graph itself as the monitored signal.

papers.title: The Cost of Consensus: When Multi-Agent Debate Underperforms Self-Correction

notes.k: Isolated self-correction beats unguided homogeneous multi-agent debate: debate fails via sycophantic conformity (up to 85.5%), contextual fragility (up to 70%), and consensus collapse (oracle gap up to 32.3 pp).
notes.r: The sharpest dissent against the multi-agent reflex; directly undercuts the debate-improves-reasoning lineage.

papers.title: Liability and Accountability for AI Agents: A Principal-Agent Perspective

notes.k: Frames the accountability question — who is responsible when the agent is wrong — through a principal-agent lens.
notes.r: Motivates interpretability, behavior evals, and fail-safe mechanisms as governance instruments.

papers.title: Bounded Autonomy for Enterprise AI

notes.k: Typed action contracts, consumer-side execution, validation-before-side-effects, and optional human approval in a deployed multi-tenant app: 23/25 trials with zero unsafe executions vs 17/25 unconstrained, both at 13-18x speedup.
notes.r: The standout production-deployment result; two wrong-entity mutations escaped every automated layer and were caught only by human confirmation.

papers.title: Pioneer Agent: Closed-Loop Continual Improvement of Small Models in Production

notes.k: Closed-loop continual improvement of small models in production via automated data curation, failure diagnosis, and regression-constrained retraining (AdaptFT-Bench).
notes.r: Attacks the cost axis from the model side — supports SLM routing without regressions.

papers.title: Token Budgets: An Empirical Catalog and Affine-Typed Mitigation of Orchestration Cost Overruns

notes.k: Catalogs 63 confirmed production budget-overrun incidents across 21 orchestration frameworks into an 8-cluster taxonomy, and ships a Rust crate using affine ownership so double-spending a budget becomes a compile error.
notes.r: Moves cost control from a code-review comment into the type system; non-bypassable under operator error in multi-agent delegation.

papers.title: AgentForesight: Online Auditing for Early Failure Prediction in Agent Trajectories

notes.k: Reframes failure attribution as online auditing: a 7B auditor sees only the current trajectory prefix and must let the run continue or raise an alarm at the earliest decisive error.
notes.r: Shifts attribution from diagnose-after-death to intervene-while-alive; beats GPT-4.1 by up to +19.9% with 3x lower step-localization error.

papers.title: HORIZON: A Cross-Domain Diagnostic Benchmark for Long-Horizon Agentic Tasks

notes.k: A diagnostic benchmark of 3,100+ trajectories from GPT-5 and Claude variants with a trajectory-grounded LLM-as-judge characterizing where and why long-horizon agentic tasks break.
notes.r: The empirical substrate for causal failure attribution at scale.

papers.title: HARBOR: Harness Tuning as Constrained Bayesian Optimization

notes.k: Argues the harness (compaction, tool caching, memory, trajectory reuse) is a first-class ML problem and formalizes harness tuning as constrained noisy Bayesian optimization over a mixed-variable config space.
notes.r: Automated search beats hand-tuned flag stacks once the configuration space exceeds a few bits.

papers.title: The Capability Paradox: Stronger Workers, Less Secure Systems

notes.k: Across 42,000 adversarial trials, stronger Worker agents made the system less secure (ASR 18.4%->63.9%, peak 94.4%); linguistic certainty mediates 74% of the effect via 'semantic hijacking.'
notes.r: Refutes 'use a smarter model'; confidence is weaponized, and a heterogeneous-ensemble defense cuts ASR from 52.8% to 2.0%.

papers.title: Knowing When Not to Answer: Abstention in Multi-Agent VLM Systems (MM-AQA)

notes.k: Benchmarks abstention (MM-AQA) and finds VLMs rarely abstain under standard prompting — even trivial confidence baselines beat them; multi-agent setups improve abstention but introduce new failure modes.
notes.r: Concrete evidence for the confidence-is-not-correctness thread in calibrated consensus.

papers.title: GSAR: Grounded Consensus via Typed Claim Grounding for Hallucination Detection

notes.k: Operationalizes grounded consensus with a 4-way claim typology (grounded/ungrounded/contradicted/complementary), evidence-type-specific weights, and an asymmetric contradiction-penalized groundedness score driving a 3-tier replan decision.
notes.r: A concrete mechanism for separating confidence from correctness in team deliberation.

papers.title: SWARM: Soft-Label Population-Level Governance for Emergent Multi-Agent Risk

notes.k: Replaces binary good/bad labels with soft probabilistic labels and adds governance levers (transaction taxes, circuit breakers, reputation decay, random audits) measured by expected-toxicity and quality-gap metrics over multi-seed scenarios.
notes.r: Evaluates the swarm, not the agent — targets risks no single agent produces in isolation.

papers.title: AMBIPOM: How to Steer Your Multi-Agent System (Human-LLM Co-Planning)

notes.k: Formalizes a human-LLM co-planning design space along three axes (mode, scope, level) and shifts supervision from outcome-level to process-level.
notes.r: A principled framework for optimal human-checkpoint placement in the loop.

sections.label: Foundations & Topologies
sections.subtopic: What a multi-agent system is as a topology, and when collaborative synergy beats coordination overhead.
themes: Orchestrator-worker, hierarchical, swarm, blackboard, and pipeline topologies
Coordination as a first-class architectural layer
Static vs adaptive, difficulty-conditioned topologies
The scaling debate: monotonic gains vs diminishing returns
Structure dictates failure mode
how_measured: Internal research-eval win rate vs single agent (Anthropic +90.2%)
Token usage as a performance-variance predictor (~80%)
Collaborative scaling-law curves over agent count (MacNet logistic; SIMAS non-monotonic)
Spectral diagnostics (condition number, spectral gap, spectral radius) vs robustness/consensus/drift
Token savings from adaptive vs static topologies (ATOM ~30%)
gaps: Reconciling the optimist (MacNet) and pessimist (SIMAS) scaling laws into one predictive model
Swarm/decentralized and blackboard literature thin in corpus (classic Hearsay-II/Nii predate arXiv)
Whether 2026 spectral/adaptive methods hold up as citations accrue
notable: Anthropic: +90.2% over single-agent Opus at ~15x token cost; ~80% of variance from token usage
MacNet: collaboration among 1000+ agents; logistic growth; irregular > regular topologies
Parks 2026: condition number is a perfect rank-order predictor of perturbation robustness (Spearman r_s=1.0)
ATOM: up to 30% token savings by sizing the team to query difficulty

sections.label: Patterns & Frameworks
sections.subtopic: The small vocabulary of coordination patterns every framework re-encodes, and the interop protocols stitching them together.
themes: Four primitives: planning/decomposition, routing/handoffs, debate/voting, role specialization
Role specialization and SOPs (CAMEL, MetaGPT, ChatDev)
Routing as the information topology of the network
Vendor consolidation (AutoGen, LangGraph, CrewAI, OpenAI Agents SDK, Microsoft Agent Framework)
Interop protocols: MCP, A2A, ACP, ANP, MPAC
how_measured: Task-completion accuracy under fixed model, varying only the wiring (MMA2A +20 pp)
Paired bootstrap CIs and McNemar's exact test on routing-strategy deltas
Code-quality differences across process topologies (Waterfall/TDD/Scrum)
Citation weight as an influence proxy for founding frameworks
gaps: Framework primary papers (LangGraph, CrewAI, OpenAI Swarm) absent from corpus — vendor docs only
Whether dynamically-recruited 'Talent Market' orgs outperform hand-designed crews
notable: AutoGen ~600, MetaGPT ~470, Du debate ~406, ChatDev ~382, CAMEL ~210 citations — the high-influence anchors
MMA2A: 52% vs 32% task completion from routing alone, but 36% vs 36% when a keyword agent (not an LLM) consumed the context
SOEN-101/FlowGen: same role agents, different process topology, different code quality

sections.label: Memory in MAS
sections.subtopic: The shared substrate a team writes to, who governs it, and how conventions and identity precipitate out of it.
themes: The spectrum of shared substrate: KV-cache, ledger/blackboard, knowledge graph, workflow, institutional
Passive blackboard vs active broadcast
Fast acquisition vs slow consolidation (Complementary Learning Systems)
Typing, quality-scoring, and pruning the substrate
Shared-for-all vs per-task specialized memory
Emergence of conventions and identity from accumulated memory
how_measured: Task success on ScienceWorld / ALFWorld / WebShop / WebArena with vs without shared memory
Memory-bank size reduction at equal-or-better task success (Auto-Dreamer 12x smaller)
Transfer across actor backbones without retraining
Governance-coverage and coordination-pathway counts in an ecosystem audit
gaps: Shared team substrate vs per-task specialization is a live, unsolved design axis (M* dissent)
How to schedule consolidation reliably without losing fast working memory
Governing a substrate before it scales — conventions do not self-assemble from raw scale
notable: Auto-Dreamer: +7 on ScienceWorld with a 12x smaller bank, transferring without retraining
UCE: ALFWorld 75.4%->96.3%, WebShop 45.1%->61.3% from typed, pruned, quality-scored memory
Theater of Mind: a naive two-agent loop 'rapidly descends into cognitive stagnation'
OpenClaw audit: 250k+ stars, 770k+ agents, 2M+ users, <=19% governance coverage, 0/12 coordination pathways functional

sections.label: Enterprise & Production
sections.subtopic: Reliability, security, cost, observability, and governance — the runtime properties that decide whether demo agents survive deployment.
themes: Security: aggregation and architecture as the attack surface
Bounded autonomy, policy-as-code, and earned-autonomy thresholds
Observability via OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions
Cost and multi-agent economics
Failure taxonomies and Byzantine-fault robustness
Distributed vs monolithic guardrail enforcement
how_measured: Production failure-rate ranges (41-87%); adoption with full approval (14.4%)
Attack-success-rate variance across configs; cross-session detector recall; violation rates
Unsafe-execution counts and speedup under bounded autonomy (23/25, 13-18x)
Token-cost multipliers and variance attribution (~15x, ~80%)
OpenTelemetry span/metric coverage; acceptance-test pass/fail and LLM-judge rubrics
gaps: Production engineering (observability, SRE-for-agents, incident postmortems) is web-only, near-absent from peer review
Where exactly the reliability-per-dollar optimum sits (verification vs cost)
Exact placement of human-in-the-loop checkpoints (too early loses speedup, too late the side effect fired)
notable: Architecture Matters: up to 3.8x attack-success variance across 13 configs; no design universally safer
Context-Fragmented Violations: 14-98% violation rates; Distributed Sentinel F1=0.95 at 106 ms vs 0.85/0.65 baselines
Bounded Autonomy: 23/25 with zero unsafe executions vs 17/25; 2 mutations caught only by human confirmation
Cost of Consensus: sycophantic adoption up to 85.5%, oracle gap up to 32.3 pp

sections.label: Frontier & Open Problems
sections.subtopic: The 2026 reliability reckoning: causal attribution, verified protocols, cost bounds, calibrated consensus, and emergent-behavior eval — each now with a named system and real numbers.
themes: Online causal failure attribution (intervene-while-alive)
Formally verified coordination protocols (TLA+)
Cost-bounded orchestration and cognitive-tier routing
Calibrated consensus and principled abstention
Information-flow control and the capability paradox
Emergent-behavior and population-level evaluation
Replayable trace observability standards
how_measured: Step-localization error and early-warning accuracy on annotated trajectories (AgentForesight, HORIZON)
Cap-violation and false-refusal counts under live-API budget tests (Token Budgets)
Adversarial attack-success-rate sweeps and mediation analysis (Capability Paradox)
Abstention rate vs confidence baselines (MM-AQA); groundedness scores (GSAR)
Expected-toxicity and quality-gap metrics over multi-seed scenarios (SWARM)
gaps: No settled answer on where the reliability-per-dollar optimum sits
Several results are single-paper, 2026-vintage, not yet independently replicated
Per-agent alignment does not compose into swarm safety — emergent risk is hard to bound
notable: Token Budgets: 63 incidents across 21 frameworks; affine-typed Rust crate makes double-spend a compile error (kappa=0.837)
Capability Paradox: ASR 18.4%->63.9% (peak 94.4%) as workers strengthen; certainty mediates 74%; ensemble defense cuts ASR 52.8%->2.0%
AgentForesight-7B: beats GPT-4.1 by up to +19.9% with 3x lower step-localization error
HORIZON: 3,100+ trajectories with human-kappa-validated LLM-as-judge attribution

reading: LLM-based Multi-Agents: A Survey of Progress and Challenges
MetaGPT: Meta Programming for a Multi-Agent Framework
Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior
Improving Factuality & Reasoning through Multiagent Debate
ChatDev: Communicative Agents for Software Development
Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail? (MAST)
Architecture Matters: Security in Multi-Agent Systems
opportunities: Causal failure attribution in agent chains
Pinpoint which agent and step is responsible when a distributed, silent multi-agent failure surfaces — and do it online, at the earliest decisive error, rather than as a post-mortem.
Formally verified coordination protocols
Model agent handoff and coordination protocols in a formal language (e.g. TLA+) and prove — or automatically repair — their safety, importing classical model checking into the agent world.
Token-budget-aware orchestration planner
Plan and execute orchestration under a hard cost ceiling, making budget overruns (double-spend, delegation fanout races) impossible by construction rather than catching them at runtime.
Durable shared team-memory substrate
Build a shared store a team can actively write to, consolidate, type, and prune — one that accumulates competence instead of slop and survives across sessions.
Emergent-behavior evaluation harness
Evaluate the swarm rather than the agent: soft-label, population-level harnesses that measure risks no single agent produces in isolation, since per-agent alignment does not compose.
Information-flow control between agents
Control what evidence and authority flow across agent boundaries so a locally-safe action cannot combine with others into a policy violation, and confidence cannot be weaponized into execution.
Confidence-calibrated consensus
Separate agreement from correctness: principled abstention thresholds and grounded claim typing so a manager never treats an assertive worker report as evidence of truth.
Learned dynamic topology per task
Learn the right communication graph per task and per query difficulty rather than fixing a static dense topology, escaping the average-complexity trap.
Optimal human-checkpoint placement
Place human-in-the-loop checkpoints at exactly the decisions automation provably fails (entity disambiguation, irreversible mutations) without losing the speedup of autonomy.
Replayable agent-trace observability standard
Standardize agent/workflow/tool/model spans and metrics so every reasoning chain is reconstructable — the substrate every other open problem (attribution, eval, cost) depends on.
Cognitive-tier model routing
Route routine decisions to cheap models and escalate to expensive ones only on disagreement or difficulty, controlling cost without sacrificing quality.
Cross-session organizational agent memory
Persist institutional memory — laws, roles, conventions, learned skills — across sessions and users so a team improves over time instead of rediscovering the same workflows and mistakes.

design.title: AutoGen — conversable agents as the framework primitive
design.task_design: Frame any multi-agent application as conversations among configurable conversable agents that send and receive messages, with humans and tools as first-class participants. Composition is the design surface: two-agent chats, group chats, and nested conversations encode planning, debate, and tool use.
design.data_generation: No bespoke dataset; the contribution is the abstraction and its evaluation across diverse applications (math, coding, QA, decision-making, entertainment) showing the same primitives generalize.
design.metrics: Task success across the demonstrated application suite; qualitative composability and developer-effort reduction. Citation weight (~600) stands in as an adoption/influence proxy.
key_numbers: ~600 citations — the most-influential framework anchor in the corpus
Now in maintenance mode under Microsoft Agent Framework (Oct 2025)
design.reusable_for_role: Use AutoGen (or its successor) when you want a flexible conversation graph rather than a fixed pipeline. The conversable-agent + group-chat primitives map directly onto orchestrator-worker and debate patterns; pair with AutoGen Studio for no-code message-flow debugging before hard-coding a topology.

design.title: Magentic-One — orchestrator-worker with a task ledger
design.task_design: A lead Orchestrator decomposes the goal, maintains a task ledger and a progress ledger, dispatches to specialized workers (web surfer, coder, file navigator), monitors progress, and re-plans when stuck. The ledger is both the working memory and the control plane.
design.data_generation: Evaluated on agentic benchmarks (web and file tasks); the architecture is model-agnostic, with workers swappable behind the orchestrator.
design.metrics: Task-completion rate on agentic benchmarks; robustness via re-planning when a worker stalls. The ledger pattern is the reference for inspectable shared working state.
key_numbers: The canonical production orchestrator-worker reference architecture
Anthropic's industrial instance of the same pattern: +90.2% over single-agent Opus at ~15x token cost
design.reusable_for_role: Reach for the orchestrator-worker + ledger pattern when the task is parallelizable and high-value and one agent's reasoning depth is the ceiling. Keep the ledger explicit and replayable so it doubles as your observability and failure-attribution substrate; budget for the ~15x token premium before committing.

design.title: Generative Agents — memory-stream and reflection
design.task_design: Each agent keeps a memory stream of timestamped natural-language observations, retrieves entries by a weighted combination of relevance, recency, and importance, and periodically synthesizes higher-level reflections that feed back into planning and behavior.
design.data_generation: A sandbox town of 25 agents; believability evaluated by human raters and ablations that remove observation, reflection, or planning to isolate each component's contribution.
design.metrics: Human-rated believability of behavior; ablation deltas when memory components are removed; emergence of coordinated social behavior (e.g. organizing a party) from individual memories.
key_numbers: ~413 citations — the memory anchor of the field
25-agent sandbox society; ablations show retrieval + reflection are load-bearing for believability
design.reusable_for_role: Adopt the memory-stream + reflection design whenever an agent or team must remember across long horizons. In a team setting, treat reflection as the consolidation step (acquire fast, consolidate slow), promote durable lessons into a shared, typed, pruned store, and keep many small focused memory files over few sprawling ones.

design_principles: Default to a single agent; reach for multi-agent only when the task is parallelizable, high-value, and exceeds one agent's reasoning depth — the ~15x token premium is real.
Match the topology to the task: clean decomposition -> pipeline; planner-integrator -> orchestrator-worker; chatty flat teams -> impose hierarchy/leadership; shared evolving state -> blackboard.
Prefer adaptive, difficulty-conditioned topologies over static dense graphs to escape the average-complexity trap.
Treat coordination as an explicit architectural layer, and routing as a first-order design variable that defines the information topology.
Verify the two-layer requirement: a richer protocol buys nothing unless the downstream reasoning layer can exploit it.
Don't ship a passive blackboard as team memory; make writes active (broadcast/notify) and build in a diversity check that breaks deadlock.
Separate fast acquisition from slow consolidation, schedule the consolidation, and type + prune the shared store like a records manager.
Make autonomy bounded and typed: typed action contracts, validation-before-side-effects, and human confirmation at entity-disambiguation and irreversible-mutation points.
Distribute guardrails and keep them stateful across sessions; re-validate every cross-agent boundary as attacker-controlled.
Standardize traces on OpenTelemetry GenAI conventions and codify earned-autonomy thresholds backed by acceptance-test evals.
design_pitfalls: Assuming more agents monotonically helps — SIMAS shows non-monotonic, diminishing returns and the Cost of Consensus shows debate can underperform self-correction.
Treating decomposition as inherently safer — Architecture Matters finds multi-agent setups are more vulnerable in most configs (up to 3.8x attack-success variance).
Upgrading worker models reflexively — the Capability Paradox shows stronger workers can push attack success to 94% via weaponized linguistic certainty.
Mistaking agreement for correctness — plurality voting can discard correct answers already in the pool (oracle gap up to 32.3 pp).
Shipping a passive shared blackboard — naive agent loops descend into sycophancy, echo chambers, and cognitive stagnation.
Logging everything but never consolidating — the shared store drowns in slop without scheduled consolidation, typing, and pruning.
Relying on session-bound or monolithic guardrails — cross-session and context-fragmented violations live precisely in the gaps a central gate cannot see.
Placing human checkpoints by guesswork — too early forfeits the 13-18x speedup, too late and the irreversible side effect has already fired.
Letting budgets be a runtime prayer — delegation fanout races and double-spends should be compile-time or planner-level impossibilities, not after-the-fact incidents.
Treating one agent's output as trusted input to the next — injections launder themselves through trusted boundaries (Cline issue-title, LiteLLM, Copilot Studio).

publication_volume.note: Pre-LLM swarm/stigmergy roots (emergent communication, virtual pheromones) that LLM teams later re-instantiate.
The framework gold-rush: AutoGen, MetaGPT, ChatDev, CAMEL, AgentVerse, Du debate, Generative Agents — the founding patterns land in one year.
Consolidation and scaling: surveys, MacNet's 1000+-agent scaling law, OpenHands, workflow memory, and the first security benchmarks (ASB, Prompt Infection).
The failure turn: MAST names why systems fail; topology-as-lever and collective-memory (SHIMI, shared-KG) mature; MCP security enters the corpus.

The reliability reckoning: an explosion of security, cost, attribution, calibrated-consensus, memory-governance, and observability work — mostly zero-citation preprints presented as emerging vocabulary.

benchmark_progression: 2023: founding frameworks establish that decomposition, role-play, and debate beat single-agent baselines (AutoGen, MetaGPT, Du debate, Generative Agents).
2024: scaling laws (MacNet, 1000+ agents, logistic growth) and the first security yardsticks (ASB, Prompt Infection) appear; workflow memory makes procedures reusable.
2025: MAST gives the field a 14-category failure taxonomy; topology is shown to be a first-class performance lever; MCP security and collective-memory systems mature.

2026: hard numbers on the frontier — affine-typed budgets (zero cap violations), online attribution (AgentForesight-7B +19.9%), the Capability Paradox (ASR up to 94.4%), and OpenTelemetry GenAI conventions as the trace standard.
temporal.narrative: The arc runs from euphoria to engineering. 2023 proved agent teams could do things single models could not; 2024-2025 asked whether the gains were real or just expensive, producing scaling laws, the MAST failure taxonomy, and the first security benchmarks. 2026 is the reliability reckoning: the field reframes orchestration as the classical distributed-systems problem of making stochastic components dependable, and for the first time attacks reliability, security, cost, memory governance, and observability with named systems and measured numbers rather than vibes.
